content: EUR/USD is the most traded currency pair globally, accounting for approximately 24% of daily forex volume (~$7.5 trillion total daily). A single pip in EUR/USD equals $10 per standard lot (100,000 units) and $0.10 per micro lot (1,000 units).

GBP/USD (Cable) has a pip value of $10 per standard lot and average daily range of 100-150 pips. It is heavily influenced by Bank of England rate decisions and UK GDP data, with spreads typically 1-2 pips during London session hours (08:00-16:00 GMT).

USD/JPY pip value is approximately $6.50-$7.50 per standard lot (varies with exchange rate, calculated as 0.01/rate * 100,000). The pair is heavily correlated with US-Japan interest rate differential and US 10-year Treasury yields, with a correlation coefficient often exceeding 0.85.

Standard lot = 100,000 base currency units, mini lot = 10,000, micro lot = 1,000, nano lot = 100. At 50:1 leverage, a standard lot EUR/USD requires $2,000 margin. At 30:1 (EU ESMA regulation), the same position requires $3,333.33 margin.

Carry trade profit = (interest rate of long currency - interest rate of short currency) * position size / 365 * days held, plus/minus exchange rate movement. Historically, AUD/JPY and NZD/JPY were popular carry pairs when Australia/NZ rates were 4-5% and Japan was near 0%.

Central bank divergence drives multi-month forex trends. When the Fed tightens while the ECB holds, EUR/USD typically declines 500-1500 pips over 6-12 months. The interest rate differential between two economies explains roughly 60-70% of long-term currency pair movement.

Support and resistance in FX markets are strongest at round numbers (1.1000, 1.1500 in EUR/USD) and previous swing highs/lows. Institutional order clusters often sit at these psychological levels with 50-100 pip stop-loss zones behind them, creating liquidity pools that market makers target.

The Asian session (Tokyo, 00:00-09:00 GMT) has the lowest volatility with 30-50 pip average ranges in EUR/USD. London session (08:00-16:00 GMT) generates ~35% of daily volume. NY session (13:00-22:00 GMT) sees the highest volatility during London/NY overlap (13:00-16:00 GMT).

Swap rates (rollover) are charged at 5 PM EST daily (triple rollover on Wednesday for weekend settlement). Long USD/JPY earns positive swap when US rates > Japan rates. Swap = (contract size * (interest rate differential + broker markup) / 365). Some brokers charge 2-3x the interbank swap spread.

EUR/GBP correlation with EUR/USD is typically +0.70 to +0.85, while GBP/USD has a -0.40 to -0.60 correlation with USD/JPY. Trading both EUR/USD long and GBP/USD long simultaneously doubles effective GBP exposure, creating hidden concentration risk in a USD-weakness thesis.

JPY, CHF, and USD are the three primary safe-haven currencies. During risk-off events (VIX >25), JPY typically appreciates 2-5% against high-beta currencies (AUD, NZD, ZAR) within days. The CHF/JPY cross often flatlines during crises as both appreciate simultaneously.

Non-Farm Payrolls (NFP) is released first Friday of each month at 8:30 AM EST. A deviation of >50K from consensus typically moves EUR/USD 50-100 pips within 5 minutes. The unemployment rate, average hourly earnings (+0.3% MoM expected), and participation rate provide secondary directional signals.

Pip value formula for pairs where USD is the quote currency: pip value = 0.0001 * lot size (e.g., 0.0001 * 100,000 = $10). For pairs where USD is the base: pip value = 0.0001 * lot size / current exchange rate. For JPY pairs: pip value = 0.01 * lot size / current rate.

The London Fix at 4:00 PM GMT triggers significant order flow as institutional portfolios rebalance. EUR/USD often reverses its intraday direction within 30 minutes of the fix. Month-end and quarter-end fixes see 3-5x normal fix-related volume, creating predictable 20-40 pip moves.

Margin call triggers when equity falls below maintenance margin (typically 50% of initial margin for US brokers, 100% for EU). At 50:1 leverage, a 2% adverse move wipes out 100% of margin. Position sizing rule: risk no more than 1-2% of account per trade, calculated as (account * risk%) / (stop_loss_pips * pip_value).

The Dollar Index (DXY) weights: EUR 57.6%, JPY 13.6%, GBP 11.9%, CAD 9.1%, SEK 4.2%, CHF 3.6%. DXY above 100 signals broad USD strength. A rising DXY typically pressures commodity prices (gold, oil) due to inverse correlation averaging -0.60 to -0.80 over the past 20 years.

Fibonacci retracement levels in FX (23.6%, 38.2%, 50%, 61.8%, 78.6%) are widely used by institutional traders. The 61.8% retracement of a strong trend move in EUR/USD produces a >60% bounce probability when aligned with a prior swing high/low in backtests across 20 years of data.

Triangular arbitrage exploits pricing inefficiencies across three pairs. Example: EUR/USD * USD/JPY should equal EUR/JPY. If EUR/USD=1.1000, USD/JPY=150.00, then EUR/JPY should be 165.00. Any deviation >2-3 pips is exploitable but typically lasts <100 milliseconds in modern markets.

FOMC rate decisions (8 per year) create average 80-120 pip moves in EUR/USD. The dot plot (Summary of Economic Projections) often matters more than the actual rate change. Forward guidance language shifts like 'patient' to 'data-dependent' can move markets 50+ pips independently of rate action.

Currency options implied volatility spikes 2-3 days before major events (NFP, ECB, FOMC). 1-week EUR/USD implied vol averages 7-8% normally but can reach 12-15% pre-event. Options traders use straddles (buy call+put at same strike) costing ~80-100 pips premium for NFP week.

COT (Commitment of Traders) report, released every Friday by CFTC, shows positioning of commercial hedgers, large speculators, and small traders. When large speculators reach extreme net long/short (>2 standard deviations from 52-week mean), EUR/USD reversal occurs within 2-4 weeks ~70% of the time.

Spread costs matter significantly for scalpers: EUR/USD typical spread of 0.5-1.0 pip means a round-trip cost of $5-$10 per standard lot. At 20 trades per day, that is $100-$200 in spread costs alone. Scalping profitability requires >55% win rate with 1:1 risk-reward to overcome spread friction.

AUD/USD and NZD/USD are commodity-correlated currencies with +0.75 to +0.85 correlation to copper and iron ore prices respectively. AUD is sensitive to Chinese PMI data because Australia exports ~35% of goods to China. A Chinese PMI below 50 typically weakens AUD/USD by 50-100 pips.

USD/CAD is inversely correlated with crude oil at approximately -0.70 to -0.85 because Canada is the world's 4th largest oil producer. A $10/barrel move in WTI crude typically corresponds to a 200-400 pip move in USD/CAD over 2-4 weeks.

Gap trading in forex occurs primarily at Sunday open when price jumps from Friday close. Gaps >20 pips in EUR/USD fill (return to Friday close) approximately 70-75% of the time within the first 4 hours of Monday trading. Weekend geopolitical events cause the largest gaps at 50-200+ pips.
